title: Ulcerative Colitis
root_mechanism: Gut dysbiosis → Epithelial tight-junction breakdown → Luminal antigen translocation → NF-kB-driven Th1/Th17 mucosal immune activation → Crypt abscess formation → Goblet cell depletion → Bloody diarrhoea and mucosal ulceration.

interventions.physiological_effect: Circadian VIP release reduces colonic spasm; cortisol peak harnessed constructively for ANS balance

interventions.intervention: Abhyanga — warm sesame oil, clockwise abdominal massage
interventions.physiological_effect: Stimulates enteric plexus via skin-gut axis; sesamol reduces prostaglandin E2; activates vagal afferents

interventions.intervention: Nadi Shodhana (5 min) + Bhramari pranayama (5 min)
interventions.physiological_effect: Nadi Shodhana rebalances ANS; Bhramari's laryngeal vibration reduces mast cell degranulation in colonic lamina propria
Recalibrates nasal-gut immune axis; reduces systemic IgE load; lowers autoimmune amplification signals
Low-residue substrate — zero mechanical irritation of inflamed crypts

interventions.intervention: Cold abdominal pack — cold wet cloth (4 layers) on lower abdomen
interventions.physiological_effect: Vasoconstriction then rebound hyperaemia increases submucosal blood flow; cold suppresses NF-kB via sympathetic inhibition

interventions.intervention: Mud pack — room-temperature wet clay on lower abdomen
interventions.physiological_effect: Clay minerals absorb endotoxins (LPS) via reflex zones; evaporative cooling reduces colonic oedema; draws excess heat from inflamed mucosa
Earthing reduces systemic cytokine load; walking stimulates migrating motor complex, preventing SIBO
Cross-leg posture promotes vagal dominance during digestion; enhances secretory IgA production
Post-prandial parasympathetic state enhances mucosal blood flow and sIgA synthesis
ENS stimulation; reduces visceral hypersensitivity; activates sacral parasympathetic outflow S2-S4
Reflex hyperaemia draws blood to periphery, reduces pelvic/colonic vascular congestion; reduces tenesmus via spinal reflex arcs

interventions.intervention: Nasya — 2 drops warm sesame oil in each nostril
interventions.physiological_effect: Stimulates olfactory-vagal pathway; trigeminal-ENS calming; reduces neuroinflammatory brain-gut axis signals
Early light dinner allows 12-hour bowel rest overnight

interventions.intervention: Cold spinal pack on lumbar-sacral spine (T10–S1)
interventions.physiological_effect: Inhibits inferior mesenteric nerve plexus (sympathetic supply to descending colon); reduces colonic spasm, tenesmus, nocturnal urgency

interventions.intervention: Epsom salt warm bath or hip bath (38–40°C)
interventions.physiological_effect: Transdermal Mg2+ absorption: Mg is natural NF-kB inhibitor; relaxes colonic smooth muscle; improves sleep for overnight mucosal repair via GH/IGF-1
Reduces nocturnal cortisol spike that worsens early-morning UC; activates IL-10 anti-inflammatory pathway via vagal stimulation

interventions.intervention: 12-hour overnight fast (last food 7 PM; first food 7 AM)
interventions.physiological_effect: Mucosal autophagy clears damaged colonocytes; stimulates intestinal stem cell (ISC) proliferation in crypts of Lieberkühn

practices.maintenance_dose: Only if urgency or loose stools return for > 2 days

relapse_rules.action: Immediately return to Phase 1 diet (bowel rest) + daily cold abdominal pack + cold spinal pack; reassess in 5 days
Return to Phase 2 full intervention schedule for 2 weeks

relapse_rules.trigger: CRP > 10 mg/L or calprotectin > 200 on quarterly check
relapse_rules.action: Return to Phase 2 for 4 weeks; investigate and eliminate dietary trigger
Full Phase 1 restart (14 days) under direct clinical supervision
Immediately implement Phase 2 protocol for 4 weeks following course completion; add probiotic-rich foods daily

dietary_protocol.meal_order: Warm liquid first → semi-solid → solid; eat in Sukhasana

fasting_rules.specification: Last food 7:00 PM; first food 7:00 AM
fasting_rules.physiological_effect: Mucosal autophagy clears damaged colonocytes; stimulates intestinal stem cell proliferation in crypts of Lieberkühn

fasting_rules.specification: Rice kanji water, lauki juice, coconut water, strained moong soup only
fasting_rules.physiological_effect: Zero osmotic load; prevents mechanical irritation of inflamed crypts; electrolyte maintenance with zero fermentation substrate

fasting_rules.specification: Soft rice congee, ghee, moong dal, boiled carrot, ripe banana, soft idli
fasting_rules.physiological_effect: Di/tripeptides absorbed in small intestine; ghee delivers butyrate to colonocytes; fermented idli introduces gentle lactobacilli

phase_macros.carbohydrates: High: 65–70% (white rice, moong, lauki — easily absorbed, zero residue)
phase_macros.protein: Low–moderate: 10–15% (strained moong, soft idli — no mechanical load)
phase_macros.fat: Low: 10–15% (ghee only — 1 tsp/meal for butyrate delivery)
phase_macros.rationale: Bowel rest priority — zero fermentable residue reaching inflamed colon; ghee provides butyrate as primary colonocyte fuel

phase_macros.carbohydrates: Moderate: 55–60% (rice, idli, khichdi, stewed fruits — introduce soluble fibre)
phase_macros.protein: Moderate: 15–20% (moong, toor dal, small curd portions — epithelial repair)
phase_macros.fat: Moderate: 20–25% (ghee 1–2 tsp/meal — ZO-1, claudin-3 tight-junction upregulation)
phase_macros.rationale: Mucosal repair via butyrate (ghee), prebiotic fibre (banana pectin), and live cultures (curd) while avoiding colonic fermentation overload

phase_macros.carbohydrates: Moderate: 50–55% (diverse whole grains, cooked vegetables, prebiotic foods)
phase_macros.fat: Moderate: 25–30% (ghee 1–2 tsp/meal maintained lifelong for NF-kB suppression)
phase_macros.rationale: Mediterranean-Ayurvedic hybrid maintaining microbiome diversity; ghee sustains colonocyte butyrate supply; curcumin maintains mucosal NF-kB suppression

items: Warm rice kanji water (strained) + pinch rock salt
Freshly pressed bottle gourd (lauki) juice — 100 ml, room temperature
Coconut water (plain, no sugar)
Moong dal soup — thin, strained, no spices
Soft-cooked white rice congee + cumin-tempered ghee + grated ash gourd
White rice + well-cooked yellow moong dal + 1 tsp ghee + boiled carrot (no skin)
Ripe banana (pectin prebiotic for Lactobacillus)
Plain rice congee OR soft idli + coriander-cumin broth
Soft idli (2–3) + coconut chutney (no raw onion) + warm cumin water
Moong dal chilla (thin, soft) + banana
Stewed apple (peeled, with cinnamon — not raw)
White rice + well-cooked toor/moong dal + ghee + 1 boiled vegetable
Small homemade curd (only if no blood in stool for 7 days)
Tender coconut water + 4 soaked almonds (skin removed)
Soft khichdi (rice:moong 2:1) + 1 tsp ghee + ajwain seasoning
White/partially-milled rice, well-cooked lentils, ghee (1–2 tsp/meal)
Cooked seasonal vegetables, fresh coconut, turmeric, coriander, cumin, fennel
Ripe banana (daily prebiotic), cooked apple (3–4×/week)
Homemade curd (1 small bowl — live cultures without commercial additives)
Ajwain water (carminative, reduces bloating)
Oats (soaked overnight — cautious introduction Week 3+)
Buttermilk with cumin (post-remission)
Ghee — 1–2 tsp every meal (butyrate = 60–70% colonocyte ATP; upregulates ZO-1, claudin-3)
Cumin water (warm, daily — carminative; stimulates digestive enzymes)
Coriander-cumin broth (anti-inflammatory; reduces colonic spasm)
Turmeric in food (curcumin maintains mucosal NF-kB suppression)
All raw vegetables
Raw fruits (except ripe banana and cooked apple)
Wheat and gluten-containing foods
Dairy (except small amounts of ghee)
All legumes except moong dal
Spicy foods and all chilli
Alcohol — absolute prohibition
Coffee and caffeinated beverages
NSAIDs — direct mucosal toxin; prostaglandin inhibition
Carbonated drinks
Artificial sweeteners — disrupt mucus bilayer
Ultra-processed food
Alcohol (all forms, permanently)
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac — inhibit prostaglandin-mediated mucosal repair)
Artificial sweeteners (disrupt mucus layer)
Carbonated drinks
Excessive raw food during stress periods

foods_to_avoid.category: Cautious Reintroduction (Phase 3 only — one food per 5 days)
items: Small wheat portions — assess for 3 days before continuing
Raw cucumber and carrot — post 3-month remission only
Lightly cooked tomato — post 3-month remission only
Oats (soaked overnight)

meals.what_to_eat: Warm rice kanji water (strained) + pinch rock salt — 200 mL
meals.physiological_rationale: Zero osmotic load; prevents mucosal irritation; rock salt provides electrolytes without triggering colonic secretion

meals.what_to_eat: Nadi Shodhana (5 min) + Bhramari (5 min) before eating
meals.physiological_rationale: ANS rebalancing before first food intake; Bhramari reduces mast cell degranulation in colonic lamina propria — reduces post-meal urgency

meals.what_to_eat: Freshly pressed bottle gourd (lauki) juice — 100 mL, room temperature, plain
meals.physiological_rationale: Alkaline pH soothes colonic mucosa; citrulline supports epithelial repair; zero fructans = zero fermentation load on inflamed colon

meals.what_to_eat: Coconut water (plain, no sugar, no packaged) — 150 mL
meals.physiological_rationale: Electrolyte replenishment (K, Na, Mg); no fructans = zero fermentation load; natural hydration without osmotic diarrhoea risk

meals.what_to_eat: Moong dal soup — thin, strained, no spices, room temperature — 200 mL
meals.physiological_rationale: Di/tripeptides absorbed entirely in small intestine; zero colonic fermentation substrate; provides albumin precursors to prevent hypoalbuminaemia
12-hour colonic rest initiates mucosal autophagy; intestinal stem cell (ISC) proliferation in crypts of Lieberkühn occurs overnight during fasting

functional_foods.name: Ghee (Clarified Butter)
functional_foods.dose_timing: 1–2 tsp at every meal (Phase 1 onwards) — cumin-tempered preferred
functional_foods.mechanism_of_action: Contains ~4% butyric acid (butyrate) — provides 60–70% of colonocyte ATP; upregulates tight-junction proteins ZO-1 and claudin-3; activates PPARgamma pathway which directly suppresses NF-kB in colonic epithelium — opposing the root inflammatory mechanism of UC

functional_foods.name: Ripe Banana
functional_foods.dose_timing: 1 ripe banana daily as snack (Phase 1 Day 6 onwards)
functional_foods.mechanism_of_action: Pectin acts as prebiotic specifically feeding Lactobacillus species; forms protective gel over inflamed mucosa reducing direct contact between luminal antigens and epithelium; pectin fermentation produces short-chain fatty acids supplementing butyrate supply

functional_foods.name: Homemade Curd (Lactobacillus)
functional_foods.dose_timing: 1 small bowl daily — only after 7 consecutive blood-free days (Phase 2)
functional_foods.mechanism_of_action: Live Lactobacillus cultures displace pathogenic species; restores Faecalibacterium prausnitzii populations; increases butyrate production; homemade preferred over commercial to avoid additives that disrupt mucus bilayer

functional_foods.name: Ajwain (Carom Seeds)
functional_foods.dose_timing: 1/2 tsp in warm water after meals; use as seasoning in khichdi and dal
functional_foods.mechanism_of_action: Thymol and carvacrol: potent anti-spasmodics reducing colonic smooth muscle hyperactivity; inhibit COX-2 reducing mucosal prostaglandin-driven inflammation; carminative action prevents gas-triggered colonic distension and urgency

functional_foods.name: Turmeric (Curcumin)
functional_foods.dose_timing: 1/4 tsp in ghee-tempered dal or rice — daily (all phases)
functional_foods.mechanism_of_action: Curcumin directly inhibits NF-kB transcription in colonic epithelium — the master switch of UC immunopathology; reduces TNF-alpha, IL-6, IL-1B simultaneously; upregulates mucin-2 secretion from goblet cells; documented equivalent to 5-ASA in mild–moderate UC remission maintenance (Holt et al. RCT)

functional_foods.name: Coconut Water
functional_foods.dose_timing: 150 mL, plain (no sugar, not packaged) — afternoon daily in Phase 1; 3–4× week Phase 2+
functional_foods.mechanism_of_action: Natural oral rehydration: K, Na, Mg in bioavailable ratio; zero fructans = zero fermentation; magnesium acts as natural NF-kB inhibitor; lauric acid provides mild antimicrobial action against pathogenic gut bacteria

functional_foods.name: Sesame Oil (Abhyanga)
functional_foods.dose_timing: Warm sesame oil — clockwise abdominal massage 15 min daily (Phase 1); 4× week (Phase 2)
functional_foods.mechanism_of_action: Sesamol (sesame lignan) reduces prostaglandin E2 synthesis via COX-2 inhibition; topical application activates vagal afferents in abdominal skin; stimulates enteric plexus via skin-gut axis; reduces colonic spasm and visceral hypersensitivity without systemic absorption

functional_foods.name: Epsom Salt (Magnesium Sulfate)
functional_foods.dose_timing: 2 cups in warm bath (38–40°C) — 20 min daily Phase 1; 3× week Phase 2; 2× week Phase 3
functional_foods.mechanism_of_action: Transdermal Mg2+ absorption bypasses inflamed gut; Mg is natural NF-kB inhibitor reducing colonic inflammation; relaxes colonic smooth muscle reducing tenesmus and urgency; sulfate is substrate for intestinal mucosal sulfation (phase II detox); improves sleep quality → GH/IGF-1 overnight mucosal repair

asanas.physiological_effect: Initiates parasympathetic dominance; VIP release reduces colonic spasm; establishes safe foundation — safe in all phases including active flare
Gentle compression of ascending/descending colon reflex zones; stimulates myenteric plexus without abdominal stress — safe in active flare
Increases mesenteric blood flow 30%; promotes sIgA production in colonic mucosa; supports rectal sphincter tone — safe in all phases
Gentle lower abdominal compression massages sigmoid colon/rectum; activates sacral parasympathetic S2-S4, reduces rectal urgency — safe in all phases
Spinal flexion/extension mobilises T10-L1 sympathetic outflow segments to large intestine; reduces neurogenic colonic inflammation — Phase 2 only

asanas.asana: Viparita Karani (Legs Up Wall) — Phase 2 onwards
asanas.physiological_effect: Reverses pelvic venous pooling; reduces rectal varicosity and haemorrhoidal congestion common in UC; lowers systemic cortisol — Phase 2 only
Reduces sympathetic inflammatory signals to gut; increases sIgA by upregulating vagal cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway — safe in all phases
Increases NO in nasal sinuses; vagal vibration reduces mast cell degranulation in colonic lamina propria — directly targets UC immunopathology — safe in all phases
Rhythmic abdominal pumping massages large intestine; improves portal venous return — AVOID in Phase 1 with active bleeding

red_flags.sign: Stool frequency > 8/day with heavy fresh blood
red_flags.action: Severe UC flare / risk of toxic megacolon — Emergency gastroenterology referral immediately
Rule out toxic megacolon or perforation — Emergency surgical evaluation required
Significant haemorrhagic anaemia — Transfusion evaluation before continuing fasting protocols

red_flags.sign: Sudden cessation of diarrhoea + abdominal distension + reduced bowel sounds
red_flags.action: Possible toxic megacolon (paradoxical sign — do NOT interpret as improvement) — Emergency evaluation

red_flags.sign: Colonic dilation > 6 cm on abdominal X-ray
red_flags.action: Toxic megacolon confirmed — Immediate surgical consultation

red_flags.sign: Weight loss > 10% body weight in 4 weeks
red_flags.action: Protein-energy malnutrition — Phase 1 bowel rest must be supervised with nutritional support

red_flags.sign: New joint swelling, skin lesions (pyoderma gangrenosum), eye redness/pain (uveitis)
red_flags.action: Extraintestinal UC manifestations indicating systemic activity — Immunomodulator review required

red_flags.sign: Patient on biologics (infliximab/adalimumab) or systemic steroids
red_flags.action: Do NOT use fasting protocols without gastroenterologist approval; coordinate BNYS and pharmacological therapy

parameters: Bone density (if on or was on steroids)

clinical_disclaimer: This protocol is adjunctive and does not replace gastroenterologist-prescribed medical therapy. All pharmaceutical decisions remain with the treating physician. BNYS interventions are designed to work synergistically with standard of care. Fasting protocols must not be used in patients on biologics or systemic steroids without gastroenterologist approval. Red flag criteria require immediate medical escalation — do not continue BNYS protocol until medically cleared.
